Proposals: 50+
Business Process Mapper + API/Automation Engineer (n8n, QuickBooks, Gmail/Drive)
Hourly: $30-$60 - Expert - Est. Time: Less than 1 week, Less than 30 hrs/week
ABOUT US We're a lean, family-run B2B steel-supply business in Houston. We source steel from mills, distributors, and fabricators, add our margin, and deliver to construction and mechanical-contractor customers. We still do plenty of straightforward sourcing-and-resale of standard material (stock lengths, 20-footers, and the like), but where we're really headed is working the material itself — cutting, threading, fabrication, and other value-added processing. That higher-value, hands-on work is what we want to specialize in, and freeing the team from manual paperwork is how we get there. Twelve years in, running at roughly a seven-figure pace on a two-desk operation that's still held together by email and a physical stack of paper. Every quote, purchase order, delivery ticket, and invoice is built by hand today. Here's the unusual part: we are NOT starting from zero. We've already built an automation layer underneath the business — n8n orchestration in the cloud, an AI agent with live QuickBooks + Gmail + Google Drive access, automatic email labeling, an email-to-database ingestion pipeline, and AI document extraction off PDFs. The infrastructure works. The problem is adoption — the team still lives in email and paper because the systems haven't yet been shaped into something faster and easier than their current habits. Usability, not capability, is our constraint. WHAT WE NEED One person who can do two things that usually take two people: 1) Sit with us, interview us, and document how the business actually runs — current-state process maps, SOPs, bottleneck analysis, and a current-state to future-state view. We recently lost a key team member who owned several steps in our order flow, so a number of handoffs need to be re-mapped against the current team. We've already gathered a lot of internal context on how the business runs — you'd build on what we hand you, not start from a blank page. 2) Use credentialed access to our QuickBooks and Gmail to analyze our real data — and verify, harden, and extend the integration layer that carries it. The data analysis is central, not a side task: getting into the live QuickBooks (invoices, bills, estimates) and our Gmail history is what gives the clarity to map our processes accurately — real volumes, real order flows, and where things actually break. Along the way you'll confirm our Google Drive, Gmail, and QuickBooks Online connections are sound, fix what's brittle, and extend coverage where the map shows it's worth it. n8n experience is strongly preferred because that's where our orchestration lives. This is a BUILD-ON-TOP engagement, not greenfield. The reward for the right person is a business that genuinely wants to adopt what gets built — we just need someone to close the gap between "it works" and "it's actually used." SCOPE & MILESTONES (fixed-scope, milestone-based — not hourly) We'll refine these with you before kickoff. MILESTONE 1 — Process Discovery & Documentation - Working sessions / interviews with the owner and operations lead to map the full order lifecycle (quote request, vendor shopping, customer quote, order confirmation, vendor PO, pickup/delivery/delivery-ticket, vendor bill, customer invoice, payment). - Build on the internal context and current-state operations overview we'll share with you — don't restart from scratch. - Deliverables: current-state process maps (visual), step-level SOPs for each stage, a bottleneck analysis ranked by time/risk, and a current-state to future-state gap view. Documentation delivered as clean Markdown so it lives in our existing workspace. MILESTONE 2 — Data Access, API Audit & Integration Hardening - Use the credentialed access to pull and analyze real QuickBooks and Gmail data — this is NOT just a security pass; the data is the raw material that makes the M1 process map accurate (real volumes, real order flows, real exceptions). - Audit the existing n8n workflows and the QuickBooks / Gmail / Google Drive connections for reliability, auth health, error handling, and failure modes. - Document what each integration does today, what's brittle, and what's missing. - Fix the high-value defects surfaced (auth/token drift, silent failures, data-quality bugs in ingestion). - Deliverables: an integration audit document (per-connection: purpose, status, risks), a prioritized fix list, and the fixes themselves for the agreed-upon high-value items. MILESTONE 3 — Prioritized Automation Roadmap - Combine the process map (M1) with the data + integration reality (M2) into a ranked automation opportunity matrix — big wins and quick wins, each scored on impact vs. effort, sequenced. - Anchor every recommendation in the documented bottlenecks, not generic best practice. - Deliverables: a prioritized roadmap document with clear "build this next, here's why, here's the rough effort" sequencing. We're open to splitting, merging, or re-sequencing these. If you think the data/API work should come before the full process map, make the case in your proposal. REQUIRED SKILLS - Business-process documentation / business analysis — you can interview non-technical operators, extract how work actually happens, and turn it into clean process maps and SOPs. - n8n — hands-on experience building and debugging workflows. - API integration — real experience with third-party APIs, OAuth flows, webhooks, and auth/token management. - Gmail API / Google Workspace, and Google Drive API. - QuickBooks Online API — querying and writing invoices, bills, estimates, items. - Data analysis — comfortable pulling and making sense of real financial and email data to inform decisions. - MCP / AI-agent tooling — familiarity with Model Context Protocol or comparable AI-agent/tool-calling setups. - Comfort handling sensitive financial data — discretion and care are non-negotiable. - Clear written communication — deliverables are documents that must be readable by non-engineers. NICE-TO-HAVES - Steel, construction, manufacturing, or B2B distribution / wholesale domain experience. - Experience improving adoption of already-built tools (change management for small teams). - AI document-extraction / OCR pipeline experience. - Airtable. - Experience documenting in Markdown and working inside structured documentation systems. CONFIDENTIALITY & DATA SENSITIVITY (please read before applying) This engagement touches LIVE business data — our QuickBooks accounting, Gmail, and Google Drive. - An NDA is required before any access is granted. - For the internal process mapping, you'll need to ask about — and document — specific vendors and customers. That's expected and fine; real names can live in the internal deliverables. - The hard rule is about documents that leave the building. On any customer-facing or vendor-facing document (purchase orders, certifications, anything shared externally), certain source relationships must stay confidential. That boundary is margin protection, not a preference — it's non-negotiable, and any process you help design has to preserve it. - You'll be working with real financial records. Care, discretion, and good data hygiene are part of the job. ENGAGEMENT DETAILS - Type: Fixed-scope project, milestone-based (not hourly). - Budget: $1,000–$4,000 for the full project, paid by milestone (split agreed at kickoff). - Start: This week — we're ready to move now. First session within days of selection. - Access: Direct API credentials to QuickBooks, Gmail, Google Drive, and n8n, granted after a signed NDA. - Working style: remote, with periodic working/interview sessions with the owner and operations lead. - This is a defined project to start. We're a growing operation actively building out our automation, so there's strong potential for continued work with the right person — but we're hiring for this scope first. PLEASE ANSWER IN YOUR PROPOSAL (VIDEO IS PREFERRED) 1) Audit experience: A big part of this role is auditing an automation/integration setup we've already built — finding what's brittle, what's missing, and what to fix. Tell us about a time you audited a client's existing system or integrations: what did you assess, what did you find, and what did the deliverable look like? If you can share a sample (redacted), even better. 2) Process discovery: You'll have live access to our QuickBooks and Gmail plus interviews with our team. How would you use the data alongside the interviews to map how the business actually runs? Share one hidden step or exception you've uncovered on past work. 3) Adoption: We have working automation our team isn't using — because it's not yet easier than their old habits. How would you figure out why, and how do you close that gap? 4) Confidentiality: This role touches live financial and email data. How have you handled sensitive client data and confidentiality boundaries on past engagements? 5) (Optional) Domain: Any experience with steel, construction, manufacturing, or B2B distribution?

Operational Mindset: "I operate five businesses and need systems that are modular and scalable." Security/Compliance: "If you handle medical or financial data, you must be capable of adhering to HIPAA/SOC2 standards." Documentation: "You are expected to provide a 'system map' so I can troubleshoot or replicate this across my other entities." Which of your five businesses is the priority for this automation setup? If it's Leader Medical Supplies, we should discuss the specific compliance hurdles for that industry before you engage a contractor.

We are looking for an experienced AI coding expert, agentic engineering practitioner, software developer, technical instructor, AI tools consultant, or builder who has strong hands-on experience using tools such as Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, OpenAI Codex, OpenCode, Windsurf, or similar AI coding environments. The instructor should have real practical experience building software with AI coding tools, not just theoretical knowledge or basic prompting experience. The course should be hands-on, project-based, and focused on real workflows that students can actually use. The instructor will create the course structure, record clear and engaging video lessons, and develop practical demos, exercises, coding projects, workflows, templates, prompts, checklists, and supporting materials that help students understand how to use AI coding tools effectively. The course should cover AI coding fundamentals, the current AI coding tools landscape, vibe coding, agentic coding, IDE-based AI coding workflows, CLI-based coding agents, project setup, prompting strategies for software development, context engineering, codebase understanding, planning with AI, generating features, debugging with AI, refactoring with AI, testing, documentation, Git and GitHub workflows, working with existing codebases, managing hallucinations and mistakes, reviewing AI-generated code, and best practices for safely using AI coding tools in real projects. The course should also cover more advanced agentic engineering topics where relevant, such as Claude Code workflows, custom instructions, project memory files, agent rules, AGENTS.md or similar project guidance files, MCP, tools, hooks, skills, sub-agents, agent orchestration, multi-agent workflows, automated development loops, issue-based development, GitHub integration, task planning, and building repeatable AI-assisted development systems. The course should include several hands-on projects and real-world demonstrations. Example projects could include building a personal website or portfolio, creating a web app, building a small SaaS-style application, creating an AI-powered internal tool, building a dashboard, adding features to an existing app, debugging and improving a broken codebase, integrating APIs, creating a chatbot or assistant, and completing a larger capstone project using agentic coding workflows. The course should teach both beginners and technical students how to work effectively with AI coding agents, while also giving experienced developers more advanced workflows for productivity, automation, and software delivery. The instructor will also be responsible for creating all supporting materials, including slides, diagrams, project files, starter code, completed code, prompts, workflow examples, downloadable resources, exercises, quizzes, checklists, and any other resources that help students follow along and practice effectively. If slides or visual explanations are needed for certain lessons, the instructor will be responsible for creating them as part of the course production. The instructor will also be responsible for delivering the final edited course videos, including clean cuts, audio cleanup, removal of mistakes or long pauses, screen recording edits, basic on-screen callouts where needed, and exporting the lessons in a polished, ready-to-publish format. The instructor must have professional filming equipment with a high-quality camera and microphone, and be comfortable on camera with excellent communication skills. The course format should primarily use screen recording, with the instructor camera visible in the corner where appropriate. For intro sections, high-level explanations, summaries, direct-to-camera teaching, and similar parts of the course, the instructor may use a full-screen camera view. For slides, demos, walkthroughs, labs, technical explanations, and similar parts of the course, the instructor should use screen recording as the main view with the camera in the corner. The instructor may switch between these formats as appropriate throughout the course. Please include your software development background, AI coding experience, examples of projects you have built using AI coding tools, experience with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, OpenCode, MCP, or similar tools, any agentic engineering or multi-agent workflow experience, teaching experience, examples of previous course recordings, tutorials, webinars, or similar training content, and confirmation that you can deliver the final edited course videos.

The goal is to move beyond simple chat interfaces and build a persistent, multi-agent operating system that can: • Handle complex workflows autonomously • Maintain long-term memory and context across sessions • Make intelligent decisions about which model to use for different tasks • Act as a proactive messaging/reply agent on my behalf • Power a full agent-built CRM and communications automation system I strongly believe in the “rent the engine, own the folder” philosophy — where the real power comes from structured, persistent context (skills, rules, memory, and standards) rather than constantly chasing the newest model. The person I hire should understand and help implement this approach properly. Current Stack • Hermes Agent (v0.16) + Hermes Workspace + Hermes WebUI on Hostinger VPS • OrcaRouter Lite as the primary smart router (with direct provider keys + OpenRouter connected) • Goal is to use OrcaRouter for intelligent 10/80/10 routing (cheap/fast models for most work, high-quality models only when needed) What I Need Help With I’m looking for someone who can work hands-on inside my environment to help train me to setup: 1. Proper multi-agent architecture (org-chart style) on top of Hermes — including planner, executor, judge/evaluator, and specialist agents. 2. Persistent shared memory system (likely Supabase + structured files) so agents can remember and improve over time. 3. Skills & standards layer — turning repeatable work into reusable, versioned skills and clear operating rules (following the “own the folder” approach). 4. Smart routing logic between OrcaRouter presets and OpenRouter Fusion for high-stakes tasks. 5. Proactive messaging / reply agent that can monitor, triage, and respond on my behalf (similar to how advanced personal AI OS systems work). 6. Foundation for an agent-built CRM/automation system (starting from a clean slate, not relying on existing GHL setups). 7. Clean documentation and structure so the system is maintainable and can scale. This is not just prompt engineering or basic chatbot work. I need someone who understands agentic systems, multi-agent coordination, persistent memory architectures, and self-hosted LLM tooling at a deeper level. Workspace → Customer → Project → Workflow hierarchy 2. Workflows with steps - A workflow is one or more AI-driven steps that execute sequentially, with intermediate state passing between them (including file artifacts — typically Excel — that one step produces and the next consumes). Each step has its own instructions, tool access, and model selection. A workflow can be triggered manually, on a schedule, or by an inbound event. 3. MCP Connection ability - The ability to connect MCP's on a per Customer level to give each customer hub specific data connections to related to that customer. Token Control and Cost Tracking - The ability to integrate with Claude to identify the tokens consumed/cost of each AI workflow or step. The goal is to control AI spend. 6. Scheduled Workflows - Similar to Claude CoWork, ability to schedule workflows for specific dates/times without a user being at their computer or logged in. 7. Workflow Templates - Templates of instructions and steps that can be shared across client hubs. The templates can be modified once in each client hub. 8. Audit Log - Full visibility and audit logs of completed AI tasks. 9. Email Inbox - Email connection (starting with Outlook) for each individual client hub that identifies emails for specific contacts for the customer and can turn those emails into tasks.

==================================================================== Senior .NET / Azure Backend Engineer — Architecture & Code Review ==================================================================== Ongoing, long-term. 20–40 hrs/week. Remote, worldwide. A ~50-person e-commerce company; you work directly with the founders and data analysts. ROLE You own the backend and data platform of a cloud-native e-commerce system — PIM, procurement/forecasting, and marketing analytics — built on .NET 10 + Azure across several repos. AI agents (Claude Code / Codex) already handle the routine implementation. We need a senior human for what they can't: architecture, judgment, and catching what they get wrong. This is not a task-execution role. If Claude can do it well, Claude does it. You make the decisions and solve the hard problems above the agents — with real authority over them. WHAT WE NEED A HUMAN FOR - Architecture and cross-repo design: data flow, contracts, boundaries, single source of truth — and what not to build. - Critical review of AI output: catching plausible-looking defects that pass tests but are wrong. - Systemic performance, scalability, and reliability design: caching, concurrency, idempotency, failure modes, eventual consistency. - Security judgment: trust boundaries, authorization, data-exposure risk. - Hard debugging: intermittent, cross-system, production issues an agent can't reason through. MUST-HAVE - Deep C# / .NET 10 + Azure — enough to judge and correct agent output, not just produce it. - Architectural judgment on Azure Functions (isolated worker), Service Bus, Durable Functions, SignalR. - Dapper over Azure SQL, strong T-SQL and data modeling. QUESTRA RECRUITMENT — AI-Powered Recruitment Marketplace (Next.js + AI Document Processing + Visa Pipeline) PROJECT OVERVIEW We're building a recruitment marketplace that connects skilled tradespeople working overseas with employers in Australia, combined with an AI-powered document processing pipeline and an integrated visa/immigration case management system. The platform is operated by a registered Australian migration agency, so data security, privacy compliance, and accuracy are not optional — they are core requirements. This is a genuinely interesting build: a three-sided marketplace (candidates, employers, internal admin) with an LLM-powered document pipeline at its core, not a template CRUD app. We're looking for a developer or small team who has actually shipped something with comparable complexity — not just "can do it" but "has done it." We have a complete, detailed specification document (11 pages, covering every module, user role, and requirement) ready to share with serious applicants. This posting is a summary — the full brief is what you'll be quoted and held against. WHAT WE'RE BUILDING Three connected portals: 1. Candidate portal — mobile-first intake flow where users upload a CV, ID document, and qualifications, which get processed by an AI pipeline (extraction, reformatting, sensitive-data redaction) before being reviewed by our internal team. 2. Employer portal — desktop-first browsing experience where employer accounts view AI-processed, redacted candidate profiles, save favourites, and request to unlock full contact details. 3. Admin dashboard — internal review queue where our team approves AI output, edits fields, manages the case pipeline, and tracks progress through multiple sequential approval/compliance stages. Plus an AI processing pipeline that: - Extracts structured data from uploaded CVs/documents (OCR + LLM extraction) - Rewrites and reformats content to a target market's professional standard - Maps job titles to a standardised occupation code list (mapping table will be supplied) - Automatically redacts personally identifying information (surname, phone, email) before any document is employer-visible - Generates a confidence score for human reviewers CORE REQUIREMENTS — READ BEFORE APPLYING This is the single most important rule in the entire build: an employer account must NEVER be able to access a candidate's unredacted contact details or surname under any circumstance, until that candidate has explicitly accepted contact. This needs to be enforced at the DATABASE level (row-level security), not just hidden in the UI. If you don't have direct experience implementing row-level security in Postgres/Supabase, please don't apply — this is the one area we will not compromise on. Beyond that: - All candidate documents (passports/IDs, CVs, certificates) must be stored encrypted, in access-tiered cloud storage - Full audit logging of every access to a candidate's personal documents - The build needs to be consistent with general data privacy best practice (we are Australian-based and subject to the Australian Privacy Principles) — prior experience building for a regulated or privacy-sensitive industry (health tech, fintech, legal/immigration tech) is a strong plus TECH STACK (preferred, open to your recommendation if justified) - Frontend/backend: Next.js 14+ (App Router) - Database/auth: Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Row-Level Security) - Document storage: AWS S3 with KMS encryption - AI/LLM: Anthropic Claude API for extraction, rewriting, and classification - OCR: AWS Textract or equivalent - Hosting: Vercel - Payments: Stripe (subscription billing) - Async processing: Upstash Redis or equivalent job queue We're flexible on stack if you have strong reasons for an alternative — just explain your reasoning in your proposal. INTEGRATIONS REQUIRED - WhatsApp Business API (via 360dialog or Twilio) for automated candidate communications - GoHighLevel (lead capture / marketing automation — we manage this, you just need to integrate via webhook/API) - A recruitment CRM (API/webhook sync for pipeline tracking) - Stripe (employer subscription billing) - An e-signature provider (SignWell or DocuSign) for client agreements WHAT WE NEED FROM YOU - Confirmed experience with Postgres/Supabase row-level security — please describe a specific project where you implemented this - Confirmed experience integrating an LLM API (Claude, GPT, etc.) into a production application for document processing, data extraction, or similar — not a chatbot widget - Next.js + TypeScript production experience (please link 2-3 live examples) - Comfortable working to a detailed written specification (we will share the full 11-page brief with serious applicants) - Available for regular video calls during Australian business hours (AEST) for at least the first 2-3 weeks ENGAGEMENT STRUCTURE We're planning a phased build: - Phase 1 (MVP): core intake flow, document upload, integrations, basic employer browsing — target 6 weeks - Phase 2 (Full platform): complete AI pipeline, full admin dashboard, full employer marketplace, security hardening — target 10 weeks following Phase 1 - Phase 3 (ongoing): ongoing feature development, open to retainer or sprint-based arrangement post-launch Open to: fixed-price per phase, or capped time & materials with milestone payments. Please indicate which model you prefer and why. HOW TO APPLY Please include in your proposal: 1. A specific example of a project where you implemented row-level security or similarly strict access control 2. A specific example of LLM/AI integration into a production app (not a demo or hackathon project) 3. Your proposed team composition (named individuals if an agency, not just headcount) 4. Your initial thoughts on timeline and rough budget range for Phase 1 + Phase 2 combined 5. Links to 2-3 live, comparable projects Generic proposals that don't address points 1 and 2 directly will not be considered — this is the part of the project that actually matters most, so please don't skip it. We're looking to move quickly with the right partner. Looking forward to your proposal.

Proposals: 20 to 50
Automation specialist
Fixed-price - Intermediate - Est. Budget: $600
# Upwork Contract Scope ## Resident Field Portal — Airtable / Fillout / Make MVP Foundation **Contractor:** Sima Memon **Client:** Stephanie DuPont **Budget:** $600 fixed price **Timeline:** Approximately 3 weeks **Project Type:** Airtable database, form flow, automations, dashboard views, exports, and documentation ## Project Summary This contract is for the first implementation phase of the **Resident Field Portal**, a structured symbolic-research data system for collecting participant experience records and organizing them into coded, exportable, research-ready data. This first contract is **not** the complete mature portal. It is the MVP foundation. The goal is to create a working Airtable-centered system that can support participant registration, PER-linked records, RFQ data collection, coding/scoring workflows, dashboard views, completion tracking, and CSV/JSON export readiness. The larger portal vision includes graph rendering, CRQ/ORF expansion, PDF reports, participant login, and physiology-data integration. Those advanced layers are reserved for later phases after the data foundation is validated. ## Core Build Objective Build the **PER-centered Airtable/FIllout/Make MVP foundation**. PER means **Personal Experience Record**. The PER is the central linked object connecting: Participant → PER Record → Program / Cohort → Form Entries → Coded Constructs → Tags / Timing Markers → Dashboard Views → CSV / JSON Export ## Required Deliverables ### 1. Airtable Base Build Create or refine the Airtable base according to the developer workbook and RF Portal brief. Required tables include, at minimum: * Participants * PER Records * Programs * Cohorts * Facilitators * Sessions / Exercises * Form Entries * Coded Constructs * Tags / Symbol Library * Timing / Cue Markers * Graph JSON Cache or graph-ready export table * Research Exports ### 2. PER-Centered Relationships Configure all key linked-record relationships, lookups, rollups, and formulas. Required relationship logic: * Each Participant may have one or more PER Records. * Each PER links to one Participant, one Program, and optionally one Cohort. * Each Form Entry links to a PER. * Each RFQ entry links to a Session / Exercise when applicable. * Coded Constructs link to Form Entries. * Tags link to Form Entries. * Timing Markers link to Form Entries and Sessions / Exercises. * Export records link to Program and Cohort. ### 3. Core Forms / Intake Flow Create the required MVP form flow using Airtable forms, Fillout, Tally, or another agreed tool. Required form flows: 1. **Registration + Consent Form** * Creates or supports creation of Participant record. * Supports PER creation or PER linkage. * Captures consent and research permission. 2. **Baseline Biodata Form** * Creates BASELINE Form Entry. * Links to existing PER. * Captures prior meditation/altered-state experience and current baseline state. 3. **Core RFQ Exercise Entry Form** * Creates RFQ Form Entry. * Links to PER. * Links to Session / Exercise. * Captures primary experience, symbols, emotions, body sensations, timing description, meaning/insight, group connection, confidence, intensity, and personal relevance. ### 4. Coding / Scoring Workflow Create a first coding workflow for RFQ entries. Required fields: * ES — Emotional Synchrony * MC — Meaning Convergence * NL — Nonlocal Impression * AT — Archetypal Parallel * TM — Timing Coherence * Optional if included: SC, BC, IC, GC, PR Use 0–5 scale: * 0 = absent * 1 = mild * 2 = present * 3 = clear * 4 = strong * 5 = very strong ### 5. Completion Logic + Automations Implement essential Airtable and/or Make.com automations. Priority automations: * Registration creates or supports creation of linked PER. * Baseline submission updates PER baseline status. * RFQ submission updates RFQ completion status. * RFQ submission can create a placeholder Coded Construct record. * Needs Review entries appear in the correct dashboard/view. * Completion Tracker reflects participant progress. ### 6. Dashboard / Scorecard Views Create first-generation internal Airtable dashboard views or interfaces. Required views: * PER Completion Tracker * Participant / Program Overview * RFQ Entries — Ready for Coding * Coded Constructs Review * Tags / Symbol Review * Timing Marker Review * Needs Review * Research Export View * Basic scorecard or summary view showing participant/program completion and construct scores ### 7. Export Readiness Set up export-ready views for: * CSV export * JSON-ready export * Deidentified research export * Program-level RFQ dataset export At minimum, the system should allow a clean export of: * Participant ID / deidentified code * PER ID * Program * Cohort * Exercise * RFQ responses * Construct scores * Tags * Timing markers ### 8. End-to-End Testing Test the full sample participant journey: Participant registration → PER creation/linkage → Baseline form → RFQ exercise entry → Coded construct record → Tags / Timing markers → Completion tracker → Export-ready dataset Use sample data from the developer workbook or create new test data as needed. ### 9. Documentation + Handoff Provide concise documentation covering: * Airtable base structure * Table relationships * Form links * Automation logic * Known limitations * How to add a new program/cohort * How to review RFQ entries * How to export data * Credential/access handoff ## Out of Scope for This $600 MVP The following are **not required** in this initial contract unless mutually renegotiated: * Full custom participant login portal * Full CRQ buildout * Full ORF buildout * NASA/JPL or Swiss Ephemeris integration * Advanced graph rendering * Automated PDF reports * AI-generated summaries * Physiology data import * HRV/EEG integration * Public Webflow redesign * Full research publication dashboard * Complex permissions system * Supabase migration These can be staged as later project phases after the MVP foundation is working. ## Milestone Structure ### Milestone 1 — $200 **Due:** End of Week 1 **Deliverables:** * Airtable base layout created or cleaned * PER-centered architecture established * Core table relationships configured * Initial fields, lookups, rollups, and formulas started * Initial intake/form architecture mapped **Acceptance Test:** A Participant can be linked to a PER, and the PER can link to Program and Cohort. --- ### Milestone 2 — $200 **Due:** After evidence of substantial development **Deliverables:** * Working Registration + Consent flow * Working Baseline Biodata flow * Working RFQ Exercise Entry flow * Essential automations started * Completion logic functioning * Dashboard/scorecard views created * Sample participant journey partially or fully working **Acceptance Test:** A test participant can move through registration, PER linkage, baseline, and RFQ entry, with data appearing in the correct views. --- ### Milestone 3 — $200 **Due:** Final handoff **Deliverables:** * End-to-end testing completed * Export views verified * Sample participant journey confirmed * Documentation delivered * Credentials/access transferred * Known limitations documented * Handoff complete **Acceptance Test:** The system can demonstrate: Participant → PER → Baseline Entry → RFQ Entry → Coded Construct → Tags / Timing Marker → Completion Dashboard → CSV/JSON-ready export ## Final Success Standard The MVP is successful when the Resident Field Portal has a functional Airtable-centered foundation where participant experience data can be collected, linked to a PER, coded, reviewed, tracked, and exported for future graphing/reporting phases.

AI-Powered Help Desk Ticket Assistant Project Overview I am seeking a developer to build an AI-powered Help Desk Ticket Assistant designed to improve support efficiency by automating common Tier-1 support tasks while maintaining human oversight for complex issues. The system should integrate with common help desk platforms and assist support agents by classifying tickets, suggesting resolutions, generating responses, and automatically handling routine requests when confidence levels are high. Business Goal Reduce help desk workload by automating repetitive support requests such as: - Password reset guidance - VPN troubleshooting - Account access issues - Software installation requests - Basic hardware troubleshooting - Frequently asked questions The objective is to reduce ticket resolution time and improve agent productivity while maintaining accuracy and security. Core Features Ticket Intake The system should accept tickets from: - Email - Web forms - ServiceNow - Zendesk - Jira Service Management - Freshservice AI Ticket Analysis Automatically determine: - Ticket category - Priority level - Suggested department - Sentiment analysis - Confidence score Knowledge Base Search The system should search approved documentation and support resources including: - PDFs - Word documents - Internal SOPs - Knowledge articles - FAQs The AI should use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ensure responses are based on company-approved documentation. AI Response Generation Generate draft responses for support agents that include: - Issue summary - Suggested troubleshooting steps - Recommended next actions - Relevant knowledge articles Automated Resolution Allow automatic responses for approved low-risk scenarios such as: - Password reset instructions - VPN setup guidance - Software installation procedures - Common FAQ requests High-risk requests should always require human review. Escalation Workflow Escalate tickets automatically when: - Confidence scores are low - Security concerns are detected - Multiple failed resolution attempts occur - Human approval is required Dashboard Provide a dashboard showing: - Tickets processed - Suggested resolutions - Automation rate - Escalation rate - Average response time - Knowledge base usage Technical Requirements Backend: - Python - FastAPI AI: - OpenAI API - LangChain or LangGraph Database: - PostgreSQL Vector Database: - ChromaDB or Pinecone Frontend: - React or Next.js Deployment: - Docker - AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud Deliverables - Complete source code - Deployment documentation - API documentation - Docker configuration - User guide - Administrator guide - Knowledge base management tools - Testing and validation reports Success Criteria The completed solution should: - Automatically classify support tickets - Suggest accurate resolutions for common issues - Reduce Tier-1 support workload - Improve average response times - Integrate with major help desk platforms - Maintain security and auditability Please provide examples of previous AI, automation, RAG, customer support, or help desk projects.

About the Role We are building a Data Operations Center of Excellence (Data Ops CoE) from the ground up. Snowflake will serve as our enterprise data lake and mart layer, fed by operational systems including Salesforce, NetSuite, Marketo, and Rippling, running on a Microsoft Azure backbone. This contractor role is central to that build-out: you will design, implement, and deliver a governed, trusted data platform that enables self-service analytics and AI-powered applications across the business. What You’ll Do • Design and implement the Snowflake data lake architecture including raw, curated, and mart layers (separate schemas/databases per domain). • Build and maintain ELT pipelines ingesting data from Salesforce (via Salesforce Data Cloud), NetSuite, Marketo, and Rippling using Snowflake-native connectors and Azure Data Factory. • Define and enforce role-based access control (RBAC) in Snowflake, integrated with Microsoft Entra ID (SSO). • Collaborate with business stakeholders to define data mart requirements, data ownership, and certification standards. • Implement data quality checks and cleansing logic, particularly for Salesforce CRM data which is a known quality risk. • Support the setup of a data catalog (Apache Atlas or Alation — TBD) including lineage, ownership tagging, and certification status. • Produce documentation for all data models, pipeline logic, and governance decisions. • Optionally support development of Snowflake Streamlit apps or Power BI-connected certified datasets for executive reporting. Required Skills & Experience • 3+ years of hands-on Snowflake experience: schema design, performance tuning, RBAC, data sharing. • Strong ELT/ETL pipeline experience — Snowflake-native features (Streams, Tasks, Snowpipe) and/or Azure Data Factory. • Experience integrating Salesforce data into a data warehouse or lake (Salesforce Data Cloud or MuleSoft a plus). • Proficiency in SQL and Python for data transformation and automation. • Familiarity with data modeling concepts: star/snowflake schemas, slowly changing dimensions, data vaults. • Experience implementing RBAC and data governance in a cloud data platform. • Comfortable working in Microsoft Azure (Data Factory, Key Vault, App Service). • Able to work independently and communicate clearly with non-technical stakeholders. Nice to Have • Experience with dbt (data build tool) for transformation layer. • Familiarity with Snowflake Streamlit for building lightweight operational apps. • Experience with Power BI connected to Snowflake certified datasets. • Knowledge of Apache Atlas or Alation for data cataloging. • Prior work in a Data Ops CoE or data governance initiative. • NetSuite or Marketo data integration experience. A Note on Data Quality Our Salesforce data currently has known quality issues that contributed to an earlier Snowflake POC being deemed insufficient. The right candidate will have experience navigating dirty source data, helping identify ownership gaps, and building cleansing logic into the pipeline — not just assuming clean inputs. Comfort with ambiguity and a pragmatic approach to incremental improvement is essential.

Proposals: 50+
Build an AI assistant for Outlook and our debt collection portal
Fixed-price - Intermediate - Est. Budget: $2.3K
We are a Dutch debt collection agency and we want to build an internal AI assistant for our team. The tool should work inside Outlook as an add-in and should also be built for integration with our own web-based debt collection portal. We want one central AI backend that can be used by both Outlook and our portal. This should not be a generic chatbot. We need a practical AI assistant with simple action buttons, predefined workflows, secure backend logic and debt collection specific reasoning. Main use cases: • Summarize email threads. • Draft replies to debtors. • Draft replies to clients. • Analyze debtor disputes. • Review payment proposals. • Prepare phone call advice. • Create short internal case notes. • Summarize case files from portal data. • Suggest the next collection action. • Support payment-oriented debt collection communication. Outlook add-in: The Outlook add-in must be very easy to use. The workflow should be: 1. User opens an email or email thread in Outlook. 2. User opens the AI assistant task pane. 3. User clicks one of the available action buttons. 4. The AI generates the result based on the selected email or thread. 5. The result appears directly in the task pane. 6. User can copy the result or insert it into the draft email. The user should not have to manually copy and paste the email thread into the tool! The Outlook task pane should have simple buttons, for example: • Summarize for case file. • Draft debtor reply. • Draft client reply. • Analyze dispute. • Review payment proposal. • Prepare phone call. • Create internal note. • Make shorter. • Make firmer. • Make more commercial. • Translate to English or Dutch. The generated text should be editable or easy to copy. The user should also be able to insert the generated text directly into a reply email. We do not want a complicated chatbot screen. We want practical action buttons that help our team respond faster. Portal integration: Our portal contains case data such as debtor details, client details, invoices, payments, interest, collection costs, notes, correspondence, deadlines and case status. The AI backend should be prepared to receive structured case data from our portal through API endpoints. In the first version, we want at least one working portal example, for example: • Case summary based on structured case data. • Next action advice based on structured case data. Inside the portal, the AI assistant should also work with simple action buttons on the case page. Possible portal buttons: • Summarize case. • Suggest next action. • Prepare debtor call. • Prepare client call. • Analyze dispute. • Draft debtor email. • Draft client email. • Create internal case note. • Review payment arrangement. The user should open a case, click the desired AI action and receive a useful result based on the available case data. Future portal data may include: • Case number. • Client name. • Debtor name. • Debtor type, consumer or business. • Principal amount. • Statutory interest. • Commercial interest. • Running interest. • Collection costs. • Payments received. • Remaining balance. • Payment arrangements. • Broken payment arrangements. • Invoice data. • Contract data. • General terms and conditions. • Earlier reminders. • Email correspondence. • WhatsApp correspondence. • Internal notes. • Phone call notes. • Action history. • Current case status. • Deadlines. • Legal escalation status. • Uploaded documents. • Dispute history. • Client instructions. • Settlement proposals. • Previous promises to pay. Debt collection logic: The AI must understand that we are a debt collection agency. For debtor communication, the AI should help move the debtor toward payment in a legally careful and commercially effective way. The AI should actively look for the strongest available basis to explain why the payment obligation exists, why the amount is due and why payment should be made. The AI should use available facts such as: • Invoices. • Agreements. • General terms and conditions. • Payment history. • Earlier acknowledgements. • Earlier promises to pay. • Email correspondence. • WhatsApp correspondence. • Delivery or service evidence. • Previous reminders. • Previous payment arrangements. • Client instructions. The AI should identify: • What the debtor admits. • What the debtor disputes. • Whether the dispute is relevant. • Whether the debtor’s argument affects the payment obligation. • What supports our client’s position. • What documents or information are still missing. • What the strongest payment-oriented response should be. The AI must distinguish between consumer debtors and business debtors. Consumer debtors: For consumer debtors, the tone must be careful, correct and not misleading. No improper pressure. The AI must avoid misleading threats, excessive pressure, unlawful statements or consequences that are not legally appropriate. Business debtors: For business debtors, the tone may be firmer and more direct. The AI may refer to escalation, legal proceedings, statutory interest, commercial interest, collection costs, withdrawal of leniency or other relevant consequences, provided these are legally defensible and commercially appropriate. The AI must never invent facts, amounts, interest, legal rights or consequences. If a step requires client approval, legal review or further instruction, the AI should present it as a possible next step, not as a certainty. The AI should help our team apply professional pressure: • Clear deadline. • Clear payment instruction. • Clear consequence if payment is not made. • Strong but concise wording. • No unnecessary legal explanation. • No overly aggressive language. • No empty threats. • No misleading statements. Interest and balance: In debt collection cases, interest may continue to accrue. If the portal provides the exact interest or balance, the AI should use it. If the exact interest or balance is not available, the AI should not guess or calculate unreliable amounts. It should simply mention that interest may continue to accrue and that the final balance or final installment may be higher. The AI should not invent financial figures. Interest, collection costs, payments and balances should preferably be provided by the portal or calculated by deterministic backend logic. Tone of voice and writing style: This is one of the most important parts of the project. The AI should not only generate correct text. It must generate text in our company tone of voice. Our tone of voice should be: • Short. • Clear. • Professional. • Firm when needed. • Commercial when writing to clients or prospects. • Payment-oriented when writing to debtors. • Natural and human. • Businesslike. • Practical. • Not too friendly. • Not too long. • Not robotic. • Not overly legalistic. The AI should avoid: • Long emails. • Long introductions. • Long explanations unless specifically requested. • Typical AI wording. • Robotic language. • Overly formal or artificial wording. • Repeating the same point multiple times. • Vague filler phrases. • Overuse of emphasis. • Unnecessary legal lectures. • Text that sounds like a chatbot. • Text that sounds like a legal encyclopedia. Dutch writing requirements: For Dutch texts, the AI should write natural Dutch business emails, as a real employee would write them. The AI should avoid unnatural Dutch accent marks such as: • “én” • “vóór” • “móét” • “zál” These should normally be written as: • “en” • “voor” • “moet” • “zal” Accents should only be used if they are truly necessary for the meaning. The AI should not use words or sentence structures that make the text look AI-generated. Debtor communication in Dutch: • Always formal. • Use the polite form. • Usually start with “Geachte heer, mevrouw,” unless the debtor’s name and gender are known with certainty. • Be concise. • Be firm. • Mention the payment obligation clearly. • Mention the deadline clearly. • Mention the consequence clearly. • Avoid unnecessary discussion. • Avoid empty threats. • Avoid misleading statements. Client communication in Dutch: • Professional. • Helpful. • Commercial. • Service-oriented. • Clear and practical. • Usually more informal than debtor communication. • Use “je/jij” when appropriate for our client communication style. • Give clear advice and next steps. • Do not make the text unnecessarily legalistic. Use of “ik” and “wij” in Dutch: The AI must distinguish carefully between “ik” and “wij”. Use “ik” when the sender personally refers to their own action, for example: • “Ik verwijs naar mijn eerdere e-mail.” • “Ik heb uw bericht beoordeeld.” • “Ik zal dit met onze cliënt bespreken.” Use “wij” when speaking on behalf of the company, the department or the client process, for example: • “Wij verzoeken u tot betaling over te gaan.” • “Wij kunnen het dossier niet sluiten.” • “Wij zullen onze cliënt hierover informeren.” Language: The tool should support Dutch and English. If the email thread is in English (or another language than Dutch), the reply should normally be in English. If the email thread is in Dutch, the reply should normally be in Dutch. The AI should not mix Dutch and English unless the user asks for it. Output length and rewrite options: The default output should be concise. Most emails should be ready to use without needing to shorten them. The tool should offer options such as: • Make shorter. • Make firmer. • Make more commercial. • Make more formal. • Make more practical. • Rewrite in natural Dutch. • Remove AI-style wording. • Translate to Dutch. • Translate to English. Prompt management: The system should support predefined prompt templates. We must be able to adjust prompts later without changing the Outlook add-in code. This can be done through a configuration file, database table or simple admin interface. The prompt system should support variables such as: • Communication type. • Language. • Debtor type. • Case status. • Outstanding amount. • Principal amount. • Interest. • Collection costs. • Payments received. • Deadline. • Client name. • Debtor name. • Tone of voice. • Desired outcome. • Legal caution level. • Available evidence. • Missing evidence. • Payment proposal. • Previous promises to pay. • Previous broken arrangements. Security: The OpenAI API key must never be exposed in the frontend. All AI calls must go through a secure backend. The system should not automatically scan the full mailbox. The user should actively select what is processed. Sensitive data should not be stored unless strictly necessary. Logging should be minimal. All AI output must be reviewed by a human user before it is sent, saved or used. The system must not automatically send emails, approve payment arrangements, escalate cases or close cases. Future commercial use: This tool is first for internal use, but we may want to sell or license it to other companies later. The first version does not need to be a full SaaS platform, but the architecture should not block future use with: • Multiple companies. • Different prompt settings per company. • Separate users and roles. • Licensing. • API access. • Outlook distribution to external users. • White-label or branded versions. • Secure separation of customer data. We want to own the source code, prompts and workflows of the custom solution. Technical preference: • Outlook add-in using Office.js. • Secure backend. • OpenAI API or Azure OpenAI. • API structure for future portal integration. • Prompt templates that we can adjust later. Budget: Our fixed budget for the first working version is $2,500. Please only apply if you can work within this budget and can deliver a working version with clear milestones.   Suggested milestones: Important: Each milestone must be testable by us as non-technical users. We do not approve milestones based only on technical explanations, architecture documents or backend setup. A milestone is only approved when we can see and test the delivered functionality ourselves. Milestone 1: Installable Outlook add-in with visible task pane, $350 Deliverables: • Outlook add-in can be installed or sideloaded in our Outlook environment. • The AI assistant task pane opens inside Outlook. • The task pane shows clear action buttons. • The UI is simple and practical. • No manual copy and paste of the email thread should be needed in the final product. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if we can open Outlook, open an email, open the AI assistant task pane and see the action buttons inside Outlook. Milestone 2: Outlook email reading and first AI result, $450 Deliverables: • User can open an email or email thread in Outlook. • User can click “Summarize for case file”. • The add-in reads the selected email or email thread. • The selected content is sent securely to the backend. • The AI result appears in the Outlook task pane. • The result can be copied. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if we can open a real email thread in Outlook, click “Summarize for case file” and receive a useful summary in the task pane without manually copying the email text. Milestone 3: Core Outlook drafting workflows, $500 Deliverables: • Draft debtor reply. • Draft client reply. • Create internal note. • Make shorter. • Make firmer. • Make more commercial. • Translate to Dutch or English. • If possible, insert generated text into a draft email. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if we can open different email threads in Outlook, click the buttons and receive usable draft texts in the task pane. The output must be short, practical and in the correct tone of voice. Milestone 4: Debt collection specific workflows, $500 Deliverables: • Analyze dispute. • Review payment proposal. • Prepare debtor phone call. • Prepare client phone call. • Suggest next action. • Output must distinguish between consumer debtors and business debtors. • Output must not invent facts, amounts, interest or legal consequences. • Output must follow our writing style and avoid typical AI wording. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if we can test the workflows with real or sample debt collection email threads and the output is useful for our daily work. The AI must clearly identify disputes, payment proposals, next steps and useful arguments to move the debtor toward payment. Milestone 5: Working portal integration, $450 Deliverables: • The portal can send structured case data to the AI backend. • The AI backend can process case data such as debtor details, client details, amounts, payments, interest, notes, case status and correspondence. • The portal receives useful AI output. • At least one working portal function must be delivered, for example: • Summarize case. • Suggest next action. • Prepare debtor call. • Draft debtor email. • The result must be visible inside the portal or through a clearly testable portal integration page. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if we can open a case in the portal, or a test case connected to the portal data structure, click an AI action and receive useful output based on the case data. Milestone 6: Final testing, installation, documentation and source code handover, $250 Deliverables: • Final bug fixes. • Installation instructions. • Documentation. • Explanation of how prompts can be adjusted. • Explanation of how the portal API connection works. • Source code handover. • Confirmation that the OpenAI or Azure OpenAI API key is not exposed in the frontend. • Confirmation that sensitive data is not stored unless strictly necessary. Test and acceptance: We approve this milestone only if the complete product can be installed, tested and used by us, and all documentation and source code have been delivered. Payment terms: Payments will be released per approved milestone only. A milestone is only approved after we have tested and accepted the functionality ourselves. We do not approve milestones based only on technical setup, screenshots or explanations. Please answer: 1. Have you built Outlook add-ins before? 2. Have you worked with Office.js? 3. Have you integrated OpenAI API or Azure OpenAI before? 4. How would you keep the API key secure? 5. How would you prepare the backend for future portal integration? 6. How would you prevent the AI from inventing amounts, interest or legal consequences? 7. How would you make the tool very easy to use in Outlook? 8. How would you make sure the AI output follows our tone of voice and avoids typical AI wording? 9. How would you structure the system so it can later support licensing or external customers? 10. Can you deliver this first working version within the $2,500 budget?

The platform will consist of two portals: #External MFI Portal MFIs should be able to: * Securely log in and manage users * Submit monthly/quarterly factsheets * Submit monthly covenant reports * Upload annual review and monitoring documents * Track the progress of their deal/application * Receive automated reminders for upcoming and overdue submissions #Internal AFI Portal Our team should be able to: * Manage investment pipeline stages * Track deal progress and activities * Monitor covenant compliance and breaches * Manage annual reviews and monitoring activities * Draft and manage Loan Review Summaries (LRS) * Access dashboards and portfolio reporting #Core Requirements #Document Management * Secure document upload and storage * Automatic organization by MFI, reporting period, and document type * Version control and audit trail * Search and retrieval capabilities #Workflow & Monitoring * Deal pipeline management * Task assignment and tracking * Automated email notifications and reminders * Covenant monitoring and breach tracking * Compliance and monitoring workflows #Reporting & Analytics * Dashboard for active MFIs * Outstanding submissions * Covenant breaches * Upcoming reviews and monitoring actions # Proposals: 50+
Re-architect & Build a Lean LLM Conversation Worker
Hourly: $15-$35 - Intermediate - Est. Time: Less than 1 month, Less than 30 hrs/week
# Help me rebuild the brain of an AI that sits with people in their hardest moments — lean, fast, and right Hi, and thank you for actually reading this. I'm a solo founder. For the last few months I've been building something I believe in more than anything I've worked on: an AI companion that meets people in the moments most apps ignore — at 3am when the head won't switch off, when something heavy is being carried and there's no one to call. It's not a chatbot and it's not a wellness tracker. It's a space that listens, remembers you across conversations, and responds like someone who actually knows you. It's live. Real people use it. On its best days, it does something rare. But I've hit a wall I can't get past alone, and I'm looking for someone who has genuinely built this kind of system before. ## How I built it A few honest words on the approach, because it shapes the job. I built this **solo and fast, with an AI pair-programmer** — iterating and stacking ideas as I learned. But the core bets were deliberate, not random: - **Two parallel memory systems.** A shared clinical **Knowledge RAG** (vetted frameworks) with **corrective grading (CRAG)** so the AI grounds itself instead of making things up — plus a **per-user Memory RAG** that learns the individual over time: their themes, the people who matter, the patterns underneath what they say. - **A "perception-first" design.** Before the main model answers, a smaller, faster model reads the emotional state of the message — the idea being to behave like a good listener who reads the room before speaking. - **Distilled memory, not transcripts.** After each conversation I extract what *mattered* — themes, patterns, what landed — instead of storing raw text. More private, and sharper for recall. - **Owned infrastructure, one model family.** Supabase + Cloudflare so I control the data, and Anthropic Claude for the voice, because the conversational quality is the whole product. The intent behind all of it: a warm, attentive presence that genuinely remembers you. Every piece was in service of that feeling. ## What it is (the stack, and what runs each turn) - **The product:** an AI mental-health **depth-conversation companion** — an *adjunct to human care, never a replacement for therapy*. In production, with real users. - **The stack:** Cloudflare Workers (the orchestration "brain"), Supabase + pgvector (memory and vectors), Anthropic Claude (Sonnet for the voice, Haiku for background work), OpenAI embeddings, a vanilla-JS PWA. - **What runs on every single turn, before the reply streams:** a crisis check → a "perception" pass that reads the emotional state → a second classifier that decides how memory is used → a four-stream retrieval over the user's stored memory → a clinical knowledge retrieval with self-correction (CRAG) → all of it rendered into a ~23,000-character context block → and *only then* the model answers. **Four model calls, ~35,000 tokens, ~7–8 seconds — per message.** ## Where I think the bottleneck is I'll give you my honest diagnosis, and I want you to confirm it or tear it apart. **I believe the bottleneck is the worker itself** — the single orchestration layer that does everything. There are two sides to it: - **It does too much, synchronously, before it speaks.** That long, mostly-*sequential* chain above — perception, a classifier, multi-stream retrieval, a structured-memory call, knowledge + CRAG, then assembling a 23K-character block — all runs *before* the first word streams. That's the ~7–8 seconds, and it's the most visible failure. - **It computes intelligence it then throws away.** The perception pass reads the moment *accurately* — and the reply often ignores it. So I pay for that work in latency and cost and don't get the benefit. The context block has also grown bloated and self-contradicting, which makes the model behave inconsistently. In short: **the system *knows* more than it *uses*, all in one heavy place.** That's where I think it's broken. You may see it differently — and that's exactly what I want to find out. ## What I need you to do Not an audit. Not a report. **I want you to design and build the lean, smart version** — a worker, or a genuinely different structure, that fixes this at the root. If you believe the right answer is a **completely different mental model** than my stack-of-classifiers-plus-giant-context-block approach, I want to hear it, and build toward it. What "fixed" looks like: - **Fast** — first word in a conversational range, not 7–8 seconds. - **Lean** — far fewer tokens per turn, a context layer that's tight and doesn't argue with itself. - **Coherent** — the AI's read of the moment actually shapes what it says. - **Right** — it recalls the correct thing, in the correct context, with a real sense of time (right now it can treat something you *just said you're about to do* as already done). I'm open to phasing it: a target design and a small proof-of-concept first, then the full build. ## Who I'm hoping to find - Someone who has **designed and shipped a real production LLM system — persistent memory, RAG, multi-step / agentic orchestration — in the last year**, and can show it. - Fluent in **latency engineering for LLM apps** (parallelizing the hot path, streaming, prompt caching, cutting synchronous work). - Real opinions about **context engineering** — knows when a big injected block is the disease, not the cure. - Comfortable with Cloudflare Workers + Supabase/pgvector + Anthropic (or clearly equivalent). ## How to apply (I'll be honest about what gets a reply) I read every word, and I'll only respond to applications that: 1. **Show me the production LLM memory/RAG/agent system you built in the last 12 months** — a link, a repo, or a real write-up. 2. **Speak to *my* situation, not a template** — one concrete thing you'd rebuild here and why, and whether you'd keep my approach or replace it. 3. **Back it up.** I'm looking for someone who has actually done this. Generic, copy-pasted proposals won't get a response — no hard feelings, I just won't. Also i will be getting allot of proposals, so at a certain point there is just that level of over-saturation. I will try my best to answer all, but usually first come first serve. Thank you! ## How we'd work I'll share the architecture and the problems openly (most of it is above). Deeper codebase access comes after we had a deeper conversation — the one piece I protect at first is the "voice," the part that makes it feel human.
moreabout "Re-architect & Build a Lean LLM Conversation Worker"

Automation on BOQ filling
Hourly - Intermediate - Est. Time: Less than 1 month, Less than 30 hrs/week
We are fire Protection Contractors trying to integrate AI in BOQ file filling method. Current Method being followed as under: BOQ file (excel file) received from the client is read by an individual who understands every line of BOQ and checks the rate of the item from the data base held with the company. This database is updated regularly as per the market rates. This individual by the virtue of his experience is able to understand the BOQ items and fill the rates. Other than the rates of the item other overheads like transportation, wastages, accessories, labour, profit etc are applied to the purchase rate to arrive at the final rates. The BOQ file given by the client is an Excel file and the make list of the items to be utilized could be in Excel/pdf format. Aim to be achieved:- To reduce the human dependency, who makes mistakes while filling multiple BOQ within in a defined time period. Wrong calculation effects the overall execution cost, directly affecting the profit/loss. Requirement:- Looking for an RAG AI agent which can ingest data from the BOQ file, identify the job given in the BOQ file provided by the client, then compare it to the item rates from the company data base and takeout the purchase cost by applying the make filter defined by the Client. Then further apply the overhead factors to give out the final BOQ line item cost and the total cost of the project execution.

Proposals: 50+
AI agent keeps looping and won't stop calling tools
Hourly: $30-$60 - Expert - Est. Time: Less than 1 week, Less than 30 hrs/week
I run a small B2B lead-gen agency, and to save my team time I built an AI agent that handles inbound leads from our website form. The idea is simple: when a lead comes in, the agent looks them up (it has a few tools — an enrichment API to pull company size, a tool to check our CRM for duplicates, and one to draft a personalized first email), decides if they're a fit, and either drafts the outreach or marks them as not qualified. It's built with LangGraph and OpenAI function calling. It worked fine in testing but now that real leads are flowing in, it's misbehaving. The main problem is the agent gets stuck in a loop — it calls the enrichment tool, gets the result, then calls it again with the exact same arguments, over and over, until it hits the recursion limit and crashes. Sometimes it also ignores the tool output completely and hallucinates a company size instead of using what the tool returned. So it's clearly something in how the agent loop and tool results are being handled, not the tools themselves (I tested those separately and they return correct data). A few guesses, though I could be wrong: - the tool result isn't being appended back to the state/messages correctly, so the agent never "sees" that it already called it - my conditional edge in LangGraph is routing back to the agent node when it should go to end - the function-calling response isn't being parsed right, so it re-triggers the same call The stack is plain Python 3.11, LangGraph, and OpenAI. I just need someone to find why it loops and ignores tool output, fix it so the agent uses tool results and stops when it's done, and add a sane recursion guard. A couple sentences on what was actually broken would be great, since I want to understand it and not reintroduce it. I'll give you the repo, a couple of sample lead payloads, and an env example so you can reproduce it in minutes. Timeline should be a day or two. When you apply, tell me in one line the most common reason a LangGraph agent loops forever on the same tool call — copy-paste proposals are easy to spot. And mention one agent or LangGraph bug you've actually fixed before.
moreabout "AI agent keeps looping and won't stop calling tools"

Process Automation Architect (Discovery & Redesign) — 4–6 Week Contract
Hourly - Expert - Est. Time: Less than 1 month, Less than 30 hrs/week
We're a national sustainability company (~30-person team) running 20+ AI agents across Sales, Finance, and Operations on n8n, Airtable, and AWS. The agents work. The problem: nobody has ever sat with each person, mapped what they actually do, and redesigned their workflows AI-first. That's this role. You'll interview every person on the team, map every task, tool touch, and handoff by role, then redesign each workflow using a three-layer model: eliminate what shouldn't exist, automate what's rule-based, and route judgment calls through a human-in-the-loop approval system. Then you write the build-ready specs our dev team executes from. This is a discovery + redesign + spec role. You don't build anything. You architect what gets built. What You'll Deliver (4 milestones): Milestone 1 (Week 1–2) — Role-Based Process Maps: Interview all ~30 team members (45–60 min each). Map every task, tool, and handoff organized by role — not by person. Deliver one process map per department + a master task inventory. Flag every multi-role person and every duplicated task. Milestone 2 (Week 2–3) — Gap + Redesign Analysis: Compare current AI agent coverage against the real task inventory. Classify every task: eliminate / automate fully / automate with HITL / keep human. Deliver a prioritized redesign report — what each person's job becomes after automation. Milestone 3 (Week 3–5) — Build-Ready Specs: Write specs for every automation opportunity. Each spec includes: trigger, inputs, AI action, output destination, confidence threshold, HITL routing logic. These go straight to the builders — must be implementation-ready, not conceptual. Milestone 4 (Week 5–6) — Audit Layer Spec: Design observability. What does a failed automation look like? What alerts fire, to whom, when? What's the recovery path? Deliver a spec the infrastructure engineer can implement. Not a Fit If You: Are primarily a developer who wants to build Have only mapped one department, never a whole company Need heavy direction to drive discovery independently Hand back current-state diagrams without redesign thinking To Apply — Answer All of the Following (applications missing answers will not be reviewed): Describe a process mapping or automation discovery project you've done — what company, how many people interviewed, what you delivered. Give one example where you redesigned a manual process into automation — what was manual before, what does it look like now, what did the person's job become? What tools do you use for process mapping? Share an example. Have you worked with n8n, Airtable, or Make.com? Describe what you built or specced. How would you interview a non-technical finance person to find automation opportunities they don't know exist? Have you ever designed monitoring/alerting for automations that can fail silently? Describe it. Your proposed rate or fixed price for this engagement (USD). Your availability, timezone, and earliest start date.

We have already ruled out the single-prompt approach. We are committed to building this in Retell using Conversation Flow. We are not looking for someone to debate whether this should be one large prompt, a single-agent prompt stack, or a general chatbot-style implementation. The architecture direction is Conversation Flow. We already have all backend APIs implemented. This project is not about designing or building our APIs. The work is focused on Retell architecture, Conversation Flow implementation, function calling design, node/component structure, prompt quality, call-flow reliability, and production readiness. At this time, we are only considering contractors who have actually shipped a complex Retell Conversation Flow or equivalent AI voice conversation-flow implementation for a paying customer with real production call volume. Prototype-only experience is not enough. We have already built versions of this agent on other voice AI platforms and moved away from them because we could not achieve the caller experience, repeatability, and reliability we need. We are now building on Retell AI and need someone with strong hands-on experience designing production-grade Retell Conversation Flows. This is not a simple chatbot prompt-writing project. We need someone who understands AI voice agent architecture, Retell conversation nodes, components, extractor nodes, function calling, latency tradeoffs, and production voice-call behavior. What We Are Building The agent will: Handle inbound retail customer service calls Support 10–12 specific use cases Allow callers to present multiple use cases in one call Use fallback handling for unsupported or unclear requests Call 1–4 already-implemented backend API functions depending on the use case Authenticate backend API calls using Retell custom headers Receive calls forwarded from Twilio to Retell via SIP Use Retell inbound webhook context to determine which business/customer the agent is representing on each call Maintain a high-quality caller experience with low latency and repeatable behavior Current Direction Based on guidance from Retell and our own testing, we are proceeding with Retell Conversation Flow. The current prototype has grown to 80+ nodes. We need help improving the architecture, reducing unnecessary complexity, and implementing best practices so the agent is maintainable, reusable, and production-ready. We are specifically looking for someone who can take an existing complex Conversation Flow direction and make it stronger, cleaner, faster, and more reliable. Retell Best Practices We Want Followed We are looking for someone who understands and can apply the following Retell design principles: Use conversation nodes for complex, multi-step, multi-scenario call flows Use components for organization and reuse where appropriate Avoid unnecessary node bloat when simpler inline extraction or function schemas are sufficient Use extractor nodes only when the extracted value affects routing, downstream logic, persistence, post-call analysis, or webhook payloads Use inline extraction in the function schema when a value is only needed for a single function call Avoid unnecessary LLM steps that could add latency Structure prompts clearly and consistently at the node level Design reusable shared functions/components across agents where appropriate Keep the caller experience fast, natural, and reliable Required Experience You must have direct experience with: Retell AI or an equivalent production AI voice conversation-flow platform Complex Conversation Flow architecture Production voice agents handling real caller volume Retell conversation nodes or equivalent node-based call-flow systems Retell components or reusable flow/module design Retell function calling or equivalent tool/function execution Extractor nodes or structured variable extraction in live voice flows Inbound webhooks and dynamic call context Backend API integration from voice-agent function calls Custom headers or equivalent API authentication patterns Twilio SIP forwarding or telephony integrations AI voice agent latency optimization Prompt engineering for voice agents Designing deterministic, repeatable conversational flows Building production customer-service or retail voice agents Strong Plus Experience with any of the following is a plus: Retail customer service workflows B2B SaaS platforms Multi-tenant agent architecture Dynamic account/business context injection Post-call analysis/webhook payload design Voice AI QA/testing frameworks Function schema design for LLM-based agents Retell deployments at production scale What We Need From You We need someone who can help us: Review our current Retell Conversation Flow prototype and architecture Recommend the right node/component/function structure Identify where extractor nodes should be used versus inline function extraction Simplify and organize the flow without reducing reliability Implement or guide implementation of the final Retell agent Improve prompt structure at the node level Ensure the agent can handle multiple caller intents in a single call Ensure calls to our already-built backend APIs are clean, authenticated, and reliable Reduce latency where possible Help us get to a production-ready implementation Deliverables Expected deliverables may include: Retell Conversation Flow architecture review Recommended node/component/function structure Updated Retell agent implementation Prompt improvements for conversation nodes Function schema recommendations Extractor node versus inline extraction recommendations API function-call integration review Testing plan for supported use cases and fallback cases Documentation of the final architecture Ideal Candidate The ideal candidate has shipped real AI voice agents into production, not just built demos, prototypes, or text chatbots. You understand that voice calls require fast responses, clear routing, reliable function calls, graceful handling of ambiguity, and measurable consistency across real caller traffic. You should be able to explain tradeoffs clearly and make practical implementation decisions based on production experience. Not a Fit Please do not apply if: You have only built chatbot-style prompts You have only built simple single-prompt voice agents You have not shipped a complex conversation-flow voice agent for a paying customer You do not have experience with production call volume You want to persuade us to use a single-prompt architecture instead You are primarily an API/backend developer without deep voice-agent flow experience
